one_line_takeaway: Moving image watermarking from pixel space into the generative model's latent space keeps robustness, cuts inference cost up to 20x, and — crucially — makes the watermark distillable into model weights for non-removable, latency-free provenance in open-source releases.
key_finding: Latent watermarking matches pixel watermarking's robustness at a fraction of the cost AND distills much better. On DCAE, post-hoc latent reaches 95.18% avg bit accuracy vs 97.78% for pixel, with a 20x inference speedup (3ms vs 63ms/image CPU). The headline result is in distillation: distilling the latent watermarker into the DCAE decoder gives 96.77% avg bit accuracy (actually BEATING its 95.18% teacher) at FID 11.48, while distilling the pixel watermarker collapses to 51.38% with reconstruction loss alone and reaches only 92.95% even with a strong extractor loss (at a degraded FID of 17.08). Distilling into the generative model itself is nearly lossless (DCAE 94.78% vs 95.18% teacher, FID even improves to 10.90; RAR 89.13% vs 90.60%). Cause: pixel watermarks are high-frequency patterns the low-capacity decoder cannot reproduce; latent watermarks are structured and learnable.
core_contribution: The paper introduces DISTSEAL (Meta FAIR / ETH Zurich), a unified framework for watermarking AI-generated images in the LATENT space of generative models rather than in pixel space, and the first to work across BOTH diffusion (DCAE) and autoregressive (RAR-XL) models. Two stages: (1) a post-hoc 'watermarker' (embedder Wθ + extractor Eθ) trained to operate on the latent representation z (continuous latents for diffusion; token embeddings before/after vector quantization for autoregressive models, using straight-through estimation to backprop through quantization), producing zw = z + eps*Wθ(z,m); (2) OPTIONAL distillation of that post-hoc watermarker either into the generative model weights (fine-tune the diffusion/AR transformer to output watermarked latents) or into the latent decoder D (train D to reconstruct watermarked images from clean latents), yielding in-model watermarking that needs no post-processing step. The paper's central empirical claim, which is its own (not cited work): latent-space watermarkers distill FAR more effectively than pixel-space watermarkers, because latent perturbations are structured and low-frequency while pixel watermarks are high-frequency patterns the decoder cannot reproduce. This is what enables state-of-the-art in-model watermarking for open-source model releases. Note: Stable Signature (Fernandez 2023, decoder fine-tuning), VideoSeal (whose UNet+ConvNeXt-tiny architecture they REUSE as the pixel baseline), AquaLoRA, Tree-Ring, and WMAR are CITED prior work, not DISTSEAL's contribution.
threat_model: Provenance / IP-protection setting for AI-generated images. The watermark must survive an adversary who applies image transformations (valuemetric: brightness/contrast/saturation/hue/grayscale/blur; geometric: flip/rotate/resize/crop; JPEG compression; and combined attacks) and still be extractable to attribute content to a model or flag it as AI-generated. A distinguishing motivation the authors emphasize: OPEN-SOURCE deployment, where post-hoc pixel watermarking gives 'weak to no security' because a user can disable it by removing a single line of code. In-model watermarking (weights-baked) is proposed to make the watermark non-removable and latency-free. The paper itself scopes the remaining attack surface: (a) watermarks can still be removed under strong attacks (referenced as Section 6.3); (b) 'watermark forgetting' — a user doing LoRA fine-tuning on their own non-watermarked data can erase the watermark from a distilled generative model; (c) 'latent-decoder switching' — decoder-distilled watermarks are defeated by replacing the decoder. The paper does NOT claim robustness against regeneration/purification attacks and flags strong geometric transforms as an open weakness.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) watermarks are still removable under strong attacks; the method is 'not robust enough against very strong attacks such as some geometric transformations' and needs pairing with post-hoc synchronization. (2) Distillation quality is upper-bounded by the post-hoc teacher, and for autoregressive models further bounded by the number of discrete tokens (RAR decoder cannot reach its teacher's robustness even with extractor loss: 90.57% vs 93.96%). (3) Watermark FORGETTING: generative-model distillation is erased by LoRA fine-tuning on ~50 non-watermarked images — bit accuracy falls to ~82%/~76% (diffusion/AR) at lr=1e-5 and ~70%/~65% at lr=1e-4 after 2500 steps; decoder distillation avoids this but is instead vulnerable to decoder replacement. (4) Only a FIXED single watermark message per in-model model (no per-inference message flexibility once baked in). (5) Multi-watermarking degrades DCAE detection by 7-8 points (though RAR loses <1 point). Reviewer-noted gaps: robustness to regeneration/diffusion-purification attacks is not evaluated; PSNR for latent watermarking is inherently low (31 dB DCAE, 24.5 dB RAR) and the authors argue PSNR is misleading, relying on FID/IS instead. Evaluation is limited to class-conditional ImageNet models, not text-to-image.

datasets_used: ImageNet / ImageNet-1k (training with 64-bit messages, 600k steps; FID/IS computed against the ImageNet-1k validation set)
50,000 generated images per model used for bit-accuracy, FID and IS evaluation
Watermark-forgetting study: 50 non-watermarked ImageNet validation images from a single class (LoRA fine-tuning)
quantitative_results: DCAE post-hoc: latent avg bit acc 95.18% vs pixel 97.78%; combined-attack acc 84.28% (latent) vs 97.29% (pixel); coverless variant 91.81%
DCAE quality: PSNR 31.06 dB (latent) vs 43.48 dB (pixel); FID 11.42 (latent) / 10.84 (pixel) vs 10.66 non-watermarked baseline (+0.76 / +0.18); IS 98.11 vs 99.53
DCAE inference: 20x speedup, 3 ms (latent) vs 63 ms (pixel) per image on CPU (8x8x128 vs 512x512x3)
RAR-XL post-hoc: pixel 97.27% avg; latent after-quantization 93.96%; latent before-quantization 90.60%; RAR speedup 2.4x (17 ms vs 41 ms/image CPU)

DCAE decoder distillation (Table 3): latent teacher 95.18% -> distilled 94.41% at lambda_w=0 and 96.77% at lambda_w=0.1 (FID 11.48); pixel teacher 97.78% -> distilled 51.38% at lambda_w=0, only 92.95% at lambda_w=1.0 with FID 17.08
RAR decoder distillation (Table 4): latent(after-quant) teacher 93.96% -> 81.40% (lambda_w=0), 90.57% (lambda_w=0.1); pixel teacher 97.27% -> 50.15% (lambda_w=0), 71.25% (lambda_w=1.0, FID 5.05)
Generative-model distillation (Table 5): DCAE in-model diffusion 94.78% avg / FID 10.90 (teacher 95.18% / 11.42); RAR in-model AR 89.13% / FID 3.32 (teacher 90.60% / 3.56)
Watermark forgetting under LoRA (2500 steps): lr=1e-5 retains ~82% (diffusion) / ~76% (AR); lr=1e-4 drops to ~70% (diffusion) / ~65% (AR) bit accuracy
Multi-watermarking: distilled RAR-XL coexists with CIN/MBRS/TrustMark/WAM/WMAR at <1 point avg bit-acc drop; distilled DCAE degrades 7-8 points
baselines_compared: Post-hoc pixel-space watermarker built on the VideoSeal architecture (UNet embedder + ConvNeXt-tiny extractor) - primary like-for-like baseline
Coverless (input-independent) latent variant, inspired by RoSteALS
Latent watermarking before-quantization vs after-quantization for the autoregressive model
Distillation of other post-hoc watermarking methods into the latent decoder: CIN, MBRS, TrustMark, WAM (Appendix C, Tables 8-9)
Generation-time watermarking WMAR (Jovanovic et al. 2025) combined with distilled RAR-XL (multi-watermarking study)
Implicit reference: Stable Signature decoder-distillation objective, which they modify (train decoder to reconstruct the watermarked image rather than only using the extractor signal)
